children.name: Naja
children.description: True cobras.

children.name: Naja naja
children.description: The iconic snake-charmer cobra; spreads hood by flattening neck ribs; potent neurotoxic and cytotoxic venom; responsible for thousands of deaths annually in South Asia.

children.name: Naja mossambica
children.description: Spits venom accurately into the eyes at up to 2.5 m; causes blindness.

children.name: Dendroaspis
children.description: Mambas — Africa's most feared snakes.

children.name: Dendroaspis polylepis
children.description: Fastest land snake at up to 20 km/h; among the most toxic; named for black mouth interior; highly alert and fast-moving; antivenom critical if bitten.

children.name: Dendroaspis angusticeps
children.description: Arboreal; vivid green; difficult to spot in canopy; neurotoxic venom.

children.name: Ophiophagus
children.description: The king cobra.

children.name: Ophiophagus hannah
children.description: World's longest venomous snake at up to 5.5 m; dietary specialist eating almost exclusively other snakes; builds a nest and guards eggs — rare maternal behaviour in snakes.

children.name: Oxyuranus
children.description: Taipans of Australia.

children.name: Oxyuranus microlepidotus
children.description: The most toxic snake venom of any land species; one bite delivers enough toxin to kill 250,000 mice; shy and rarely encountered; remote black-soil plains of central Queensland.

children.name: Pseudonaja
children.description: Eastern brown snakes.

children.name: Pseudonaja textilis
children.description: Responsible for most snakebite deaths in Australia; common in agricultural areas; fast and defensive when provoked.

children.name: Notechis
children.description: Tiger snakes.

children.name: Notechis scutatus
children.description: Vivid banding; highly venomous; common near wetlands in southern Australia; procoagulant venom causes blood to clot uncontrollably.

children.name: Bungarus
children.description: Asian kraits.

children.name: Bungarus caeruleus
children.description: Nocturnal; responsible for many deaths in South Asia; neurotoxic venom acts fast; victims often feel only a pinprick then sleep and may not wake.

children.name: Bungarus multicinctus
children.description: Black and white bands; highly toxic; bungarotoxin used widely in neuroscience research.

children.name: Laticauda
children.description: Amphibious sea kraits.

children.name: Laticauda colubrina
children.description: Amphibious; comes ashore to digest prey and lay eggs; potent venom but extremely docile; key prey for sea eagles on Pacific islands.

children.name: Hydrophis
children.description: True sea snakes.

children.name: Hydrophis platurus
children.description: Widest geographic range of any non-marine snake; pelagic; cannot move on land at all; gives birth at sea.
